title: Cisco Standard ACLs
description: Learn to create and apply standard IP access control lists on Cisco IOS devices, including permit/deny rules, wildcard masks, remarks, and interface binding.

tasks.prompt: Create standard ACL 10 to permit host 192.168.1.50.
tasks.explanation: Standard ACLs filter traffic based solely on source IP address. The 'host' keyword matches a single specific address.

tasks.prompt: Add a deny rule to ACL 10 blocking host 192.168.1.99.
tasks.explanation: Deny statements in a standard ACL block traffic from the specified source address. Order matters — place specific denies before broad permits.

tasks.prompt: Permit all hosts on network 192.168.1.0/24 in standard ACL 10.
tasks.explanation: Standard ACLs use a wildcard mask to match a range of source addresses. 0.0.0.255 matches all hosts in the 192.168.1.0/24 subnet.

tasks.prompt: Add a permit any rule to standard ACL 10 to allow all remaining traffic.
tasks.explanation: The 'permit any' keyword matches all source addresses. An implicit deny-all exists at the end of every ACL if no explicit permit is present.

tasks.prompt: Add a remark to ACL 10 describing it as 'Block unauthorized hosts'.
tasks.explanation: ACL remarks are documentation comments embedded in the access list to help administrators understand each rule's purpose.

tasks.prompt: Apply standard ACL 10 inbound on interface GigabitEthernet0/0.
tasks.explanation: Binding an ACL inbound filters traffic as it enters the interface. Standard ACLs should be placed as close to the destination as possible.

tasks.prompt: Apply standard ACL 10 outbound on interface GigabitEthernet0/1.
tasks.explanation: An outbound ACL filters traffic leaving the interface. Only one ACL per direction can be applied to an interface at a time.

tasks.prompt: Remove standard ACL 10 entirely from the router configuration.
tasks.explanation: The 'no access-list' command deletes the entire numbered ACL. Remember to remove it from any interfaces first to avoid orphaned references.

tasks.prompt: Display all configured access lists and their hit counters.
tasks.explanation: This command shows each ACL entry along with match counters, helping verify which rules are being hit by live traffic.

tasks.prompt: Verify which ACL is applied on interface GigabitEthernet0/0.
tasks.explanation: The show ip interface command confirms ACL bindings and displays inbound/outbound access list assignments on a specific interface.
